texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We analyze the finite-blocklength performance of lossy joint source-channel codes (JSCC) in an unknown-channel framework, where the true channel is unknown but the source distribution is known. We establish achievability results for mismatched-design JSCC, where the code design is based on a channel Q Y | X but deployed over a different channel P Y | X . Our mismatched-design achievability result allows nonstationary channel laws and arbitrary standard Borel alphabets for the source, reproduction, channel input and channel output. The achievability bound is given in terms of the rate-distortion and rate-dispersion functions, as well as two channel-dependent quantities that we call the mismatched-design rate and mismatched-design rate-dispersion. For block erasure channels, our result shows that channel mismatch incurs no penalty. We then show a second-order universal family of source-channel codes over the set of block erasure channels. Our code construction uses Poisson functional representations of suitable conditional probability measures to produce the encoder and decoder outputs. We use a parameterized family of Gibbs posteriors as the decoder-side kernels, whose envelope recovers the generalized mutual information.

Our mismatched-design achievability result allows nonstationary channel laws and arbitrary standard Borel alphabets for the source, reproduction, channel input and channel output. The achievability bound is given in terms of the rate-distortion and rate-dispersion functions, as well as two channel-dependent quantities that we call the mismatched-design rate and mismatched-design rate-dispersion. For block erasure channels, our result shows that channel mismatch incurs no penalty. We then show a second-order universal family of source-channel codes over the set of block erasure channels. Our code construction uses Poisson functional representations of suitable conditional probability measures to produce the encoder and decoder outputs. We use a parameterized family of Gibbs posteriors as the decoder-side kernels, whose envelope recovers the generalized mutual information.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With the advent of AI, joint source-channel coding (JSCC) has attracted significant recent interest [1]-[4]. In AI-based JSCC, encoder models are trained to map source data directly to channel inputs, while decoder models are trained to reconstruct the source data with the highest possible fidelity. Image and video datasets, combined with synthetic additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) channels, are commonly used for training. Prior studies have shown that encoder and decoder models trained at a single nominal channel signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) can perform reasonably well over a range of SNR values [2], [3]. This behavior contrasts with separate source and channel coding, which exhibits a waterfall effect, characterized by a sharp degradation in reconstruction fidelity when the channel capacity falls below the source encoder rate. These findings suggest that JSCC may offer robustness to channel uncertainty. Motivated by this observation, in this paper, we investigate achievable performance bounds for lossy JSCC when the source distribution is assumed to be known but the actual channel statistics are unknown to both the encoder and the decoder.
texts.text: With the advent of AI, joint source-channel coding (JSCC) has attracted significant recent interest [1]-[4]. In AI-based JSCC, encoder models are trained to map source data directly to channel inputs, while decoder models are trained to reconstruct the source data with the highest possible fidelity. Image and video datasets, combined with synthetic additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) channels, are commonly used for training. Prior studies have shown that encoder and decoder models trained at a single nominal channel signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) can perform reasonably well over a range of SNR values [2], [3]. This behavior contrasts with separate source and channel coding, which exhibits a waterfall effect, characterized by a sharp degradation in reconstruction fidelity when the channel capacity falls below the source encoder rate. These findings suggest that JSCC may offer robustness to channel uncertainty. Motivated by this observation, in this paper, we investigate achievable performance bounds for lossy JSCC when the source distribution is assumed to be known but the actual channel statistics are unknown to both the encoder and the decoder.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let P Y | X denote the unknown channel, which we will also call the true channel. In this unknown-channel framework, we study a mismatched-design class of JSCC in which the JSCC design is based on a channel Q Y | X fi xed at design time. Both the channel input distribution Q ∗ X and the decoder kernel are based on Q Y | X , while the parameters of P Y | X play no role in the JSCC construction. We call this a Q Y | X -based source-channel code. Through refined asymptotic analysis based on the normal approximation, we analyze the finite-blocklength performance of the Q Y | X -based source-channel code when the code is run over the channel P Y | X . Our results characterizing an achievable performance region under the channel mismatch will be called mismatched-design results.

Our results characterizing an achievable performance region under the channel mismatch will be called mismatched-design results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Such mismatched-design JSCC for unknown channels is driven by the needs of real deployments. On the one hand, for transmission over mobile channels, only an estimate Q Y | X of the true channel P Y | X may be known, so that the JSCC design may be Q Y | X -based. On the other hand, replacing separate source and channel coding by JSCC in real communication networks is difficult [5] because practical networks are inherently modular: application providers responsible for source coding and network providers responsible for channel coding are typically distinct entities connected through standardized packet interfaces. It is impractical to relay the real-time channel state information to the application provider to enable channel-aware source coding.

It is impractical to relay the real-time channel state information to the application provider to enable channel-aware source coding.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent works [5], [6] proposed a new standard interface, called the multi-level reliability interface, between the application layer and the network in order to abstract the network channel into multiple reliability levels for the application provider. The source coder at the application layer is then trained as a JSCC for a channel abstraction Q Y | X derived from the multilevel reliability interface. The channel coder at the network optimizes the transmission of the message packets based on packet importance weights [7] that are also derived from the same interface. The channel abstraction used for training at the application is chosen to be block erasure channels (BECs) with different erasure probabilities in [6]. The motivation for this choice is that in real packet networks, decoding failures are naturally represented as packet erasures, with the decoder explicitly knowing which blocks/packets are missing. Hence, the true channel P Y | X can be modeled as a nonstationary BEC, with different packet erasure probabilities that are determined by factors such as channel conditions and scheduling policies. Understanding the achievable finite-blocklength performance in the channel-mismatched framework provides valuable insights in optimizing the design and operation: the application uses a JSCC trained for a channel abstraction Q Y | X while the network chooses its

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To derive our mismatched-design result, we consider a specific construction of a Q Y | X -based JSCC and analyze its achievable performance over a different channel P Y | X . We focus on a complete channel-mismatched setting where the parameters of the true (unknown) channel P Y | X play no role in the JSCC construction. In particular, this makes the problem different from the analysis of mismatch capacity in the mismatched decoding framework [8], which entails optimizing over the channel input law while the decoding metric is arbitrary but fixed. Our JSCC construction departs from the conventional framework of shared i.i.d. source and channel codebooks between the encoder and decoder. Instead, following [9], we have a Poisson point process as shared randomness, and the encoder and decoder obtain the channel input and source reconstruction, respectively, using the Poisson functional representation [9, Definition 1] of suitable conditional probability measures. The source distribution P S is assumed to be fully known at design time, so this paper does not focus on source mismatch.

Instead, following [9], we have a Poisson point process as shared randomness, and the encoder and decoder obtain the channel input and source reconstruction, respectively, using the Poisson functional representation [9, Definition 1] of suitable conditional probability measures. The source distribution P S is assumed to be fully known at design time, so this paper does not focus on source mismatch.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our one-shot results in the mismatched-design framework encompass arbitrary sources and channels on standard Borel spaces, albeit with certain technical assumptions which ensure, in particular, that the information density of the joint distribution Q ∗ X × Q Y | X is well-defined under the mismatched distribution Q ∗ X × P Y | X . On the other hand, our block coding results based on the normal approximation encompass memoryless, stationary sources and memoryless, but not necessarily stationary, channels, whose single-letter probability laws are arbitrary but satisfy mild regularity conditions. We prove finite-blocklength achievability bounds in the memoryless setting in terms of the usual source-related quantities, the rate-distortion and ratedispersion functions [10], as well as two channel-related single-letter quantities, which we call the mismatched-design rate and the mismatched-design rate-dispersion . Mismatched-design performance outside of the channel regularity conditions is discussed from an operational perspective.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For block erasure channels (BECs), the mismatched-design rate and mismatched-design rate-dispersion are simply the usual capacity and dispersion of the true block erasure channel P Y | X as long as Q Y | X has an erasure probability in (0 , 1) , thus proving that there is no penalty of channel mismatch for BECs. We use this observation to formalize a notion of second-order universal source-channel codes and then construct such codes over the set of block erasure channels. At a high level, a JSCC scheme is second-order channel-universal over a set of channels W if its encoder and decoder do not depend on the true P Y | X ∈ W , yet for every P Y | X ∈ W , it achieves the same first- and second-order asymptotic performance as the P Y | X -matched optimal JSCC scheme. This universal result should be understood as a property of a family of codes, indexed by the channel blocklength n and source blocklength k . Our universal results do not imply the existence of a single source-channel code that is optimal for every channel in a given set W . This is because a single source-channel code C n,k in the family is associated with an operating point ( n, k ) and also depends on the target distortion level d . Then for a certain channel P Y | X in a channel set W , the code C n,k might be optimal in the sense that the rate R = k/n is the best achievable rate for that channel and the distortion level d , while for other channels in W , a higher rate might be achievable, making the code C n,k suboptimal. We make the notion of universality rigorous in Section IV-A and present our universal results in Section IV-B.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A. Related Work
texts.text: A. Related Work

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mismatched-design frameworks have been studied in several works under a variety of closely related formulations and mostly in the context of channel coding. Second-order asymptotics of the achievable rate based on the normal approximation were studied in [11] for the case when a channel code optimized for AWGN channels (i.e., one that uses spherical codebooks and nearest neighbor decoding) is deployed over arbitrary additive (non-Gaussian) noise memoryless channels; first-order results for this same framework were previously derived by Lapidoth [12]. The 'mismatch' in [11] and [12] is that between a Gaussian model and a non-Gaussian model for the additive noise in the channel. However, most works on mismatched-design frameworks have focused on mismatched decoding in the context of channel coding, where the decoding rule is the argmax of a fixed, possibly suboptimal, decoding metric. In this mismatched maximum-metric decoding formulation, first-order asymptotically achievable rates given by the generalized mutual information (GMI) and the lower bound on mismatched (LM) capacity have been derived; see the survey paper [13] and references therein. In particular, [8] derives lower bounds to the mismatch capacity in several settings, which include general alphabet channels with memory and non-single-letter decoding metrics. Second-order asymptotics of achievable rates (second-order coding rates) with mismatched decoding are studied in [14], featuring the GMI and the LM rate together with the dispersion as the second-order term in the asymptotic expansion [14, Theorem 5].

The 'mismatch' in [11] and [12] is that between a Gaussian model and a non-Gaussian model for the additive noise in the channel. However, most works on mismatched-design frameworks have focused on mismatched decoding in the context of channel coding, where the decoding rule is the argmax of a fixed, possibly suboptimal, decoding metric. In this mismatched maximum-metric decoding formulation, first-order asymptotically achievable rates given by the generalized mutual information (GMI) and the lower bound on mismatched (LM) capacity have been derived; see the survey paper [13] and references therein. In particular, [8] derives lower bounds to the mismatch capacity in several settings, which include general alphabet channels with memory and non-single-letter decoding metrics. Second-order asymptotics of achievable rates (second-order coding rates) with mismatched decoding are studied in [14], featuring the GMI and the LM rate together with the dispersion as the second-order term in the asymptotic expansion [14, Theorem 5].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our mismatched-design formulation is closer in spirit to the mismatched-decoding setting of [8], [13] and [14], but differs in several aspects. First, we derive results for JSCC rather than for channel coding, which requires proof techniques different from the random-coding union bound used in [14, Theorem 1]. Second, we restrict our attention to a parameterized family of decoding metrics induced by the design channel Q Y | X rather than completely arbitrary decoding metrics. However, this restriction is not a limitation of our proof techniques; the same methods can be extended to arbitrary decoding metrics. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Literature on mismatched JSCC frameworks is relatively sparse, especially in the finite-blocklength regime. Optimal mismatched analysis, or even standard matched analysis, in JSCC in the finite-blocklength regime is harder than in channel coding because an optimal JSCC decoder in the finite-blocklength regime does not generally reduce to a maximum likelihood channel decoder [15, p. 2552]. Zhou, Tan and Motani [16] studied a JSCC architecture called NN-JSCC that is constrained to use random codebooks (for both source reproduction and channel codewords) that are either i.i.d. Gaussian or spherical. However, instead of nearest neighbor encoding and decoding rules that are optimal for Gaussian sources and channels, they use modified minimum distance and nearest neighbor schemes that serve to maximize the communication rate when transmitting an unknown arbitrary memoryless source over an unknown arbitrary additive noise memoryless channel, although the source power and noise power are known and fixed. Such modifications ensure a robust performance over a range of source and channel laws; a naive JSCC construction designed optimally only for Gaussian sources and channels (i.e., one that uses plain nearest neighbor schemes) may not necessarily perform well for arbitrarily distributed sources and additive channels.

Gaussian or spherical. However, instead of nearest neighbor encoding and decoding rules that are optimal for Gaussian sources and channels, they use modified minimum distance and nearest neighbor schemes that serve to maximize the communication rate when transmitting an unknown arbitrary memoryless source over an unknown arbitrary additive noise memoryless channel, although the source power and noise power are known and fixed. Such modifications ensure a robust performance over a range of source and channel laws; a naive JSCC construction designed optimally only for Gaussian sources and channels (i.e., one that uses plain nearest neighbor schemes) may not necessarily perform well for arbitrarily distributed sources and additive channels.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The NN-JSCC is part of the mismatched-design class of JSCC. Indeed, the fact that the NN-JSCC uses random codebooks optimized for Gaussian channels represents exactly a channel-mismatch design because such codebooks are not necessarily optimal for general additive non-Gaussian noise channels. Hence, results for NN-JSCC characterize the performance under mismatch between a Gaussian model and a non-Gaussian model for the source and channel, while the source power and noise power are matched and known. Furthermore, when the true source and channel are Gaussian, the NN-JSCC achieves the optimal first- and second-order performance. However, the performance of NN-JSCC is not shown to be optimal for an arbitrary non-Gaussian source and an additive non-Gaussian noise channel. Hence, it is not clear whether NN-JSCC can be said to be a universal construction over any non-trivial set of channels, especially since the NN-JSCC design is based on a fixed and known channel noise power [16, (2)]. Nevertheless, the NN-JSCC remains 'robust' in the sense that for any arbitrary source with fixed source power (or second-moment) σ 2 and an additive channel with fixed SNR P , the first-order asymptotic rate (number of source symbols per channel use) achieved by the NN-JSCC is C ( P ) /R d ( σ 2 ) , where C ( P ) and R d ( σ 2 ) are the Gaussian capacity and rate-distortion function, respectively. A similar notion of robustness was proved in [12] for channel codes and in [17] for lossy source codes. In [12], the rate C ( P ) was shown to be achievable over all additive (non-Gaussian) channels with SNR P using a channel code based on Gaussian codebook and nearest neighbor decoding. In [17], the compression rate R d ( σ 2 ) was shown to be achievable for all (non-Gaussian) sources with second-moment σ 2 using a source code based on a Gaussian codebook. However, universality is a stronger notion than robustness.

texts.orig: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section II-A provides the notation, definitions and regularity assumptions that hold throughout the paper. Section II-B provides the general one-shot JSCC construction as well as the specialization of the general one-shot construction to obtain a parameterized family of Q Y | X -based source-channel codes. Section II-C provides the definitions of mismatched-design rate and mismatched-design rate-dispersion. Section III presents the mismatched-design results in the memoryless block coding framework for Q Y | X -based source-channel codes when the true (unknown) channel is P Y | X . Section IV gives the definition of second-order universality and constructs a second-order universal family of sourcechannel codes over the set of block erasure channels. Section V gives the concluding remarks and possible future directions. Appendices A-E contain the technical proofs, while Appendix F provides a few examples of source distributions that satisfy the regularity conditions of our main results.

texts.orig: Let S , X , Y and Z be the source, channel input, channel output and source reconstruction alphabets, respectively. Focusing on the one-shot paradigm, we will let these alphabets be arbitrary standard Borel spaces, with no imposition of a Cartesian product structure. We will write B ( S ) to denote the Borel σ -algebra on S . When needed for certain results later in the paper, we will instantiate the alphabets as n -fold Cartesian products.
texts.text: Let S , X , Y and Z be the source, channel input, channel output and source reconstruction alphabets, respectively. Focusing on the one-shot paradigm, we will let these alphabets be arbitrary standard Borel spaces, with no imposition of a Cartesian product structure. We will write B ( S ) to denote the Borel σ -algebra on S . When needed for certain results later in the paper, we will instantiate the alphabets as n -fold Cartesian products.

texts.orig: · A necessary condition for the well-definedness of the RHS of (15) is P ∗ Y ≪ Q ∗ Y , where Q ∗ Y = Q ∗ X Q Y | X . This condition is implied by (14) . From an operational perspective, it is reasonable to set C t ( P Y | X ∥ Q Y | X ) to zero for the case when P ∗ Y ̸≪ Q ∗ Y . For simplicity, we assume finite alphabets to describe this operational justification. If P ∗ Y ̸≪ Q ∗ Y , then there exists a set of outputs, call it A ⊂ Y , such that Q ∗ Y ( A ) = 0 but P ∗ Y ( A ) > 0 . For all y ∈ A , Q Y | X ( y | x ) = 0 for all x ∈ supp( Q ∗ X ) . This means that observing a channel output y ∈ A , the decoding rule in the ( t, Q Y | X ) -based code (see Definition 3) can only rely on arbitrary tie-breaking and output an arbitrary reconstruction, with the channel output y being essentially ignored. Because P ∗ Y ( A ) > 0 , such outputs will occur with asymptotically non-negligible probability in the block coding setting under the true channel P Y | X , and for Q ∗ X -typical transmitted codewords they occur with probability tending to one. Consequently, the excess-distortion probability cannot vanish at any positive rate. A similar reasoning applies if the Q Y | X -based source-channel code had a conventional Q Y | X -based MAP decoder instead of one based on Poisson functional representation.

texts.orig: First, note that if P ( i ) Y | X = Q ( i ) Y | X for all i , then Theorem 1 is simply an achievability result for traditional matched JSCC with known channel laws. In this case, Theorem 1 extends the achievability half of [15, Theorem 10] to nonstationary channels. If, in addition, P ( i ) Y | X = Q ( i ) Y | X = P Y | X for all i , then Theorem 1 essentially recovers the achievability half of [15, Theorem 10] with a slight improvement since [15, Theorem 10] contains an extra log log n term in the RHS of (30) . This improvement, however, was first observed in [9, Appendix D].

texts.orig: We also remark that there is a notable difference between the achievability of (32) by the ( t, Q Y | X ) -based JSCC that we show in Theorem 1 and by a channel code with a fixed Q Y | X -based maximum likelihood (ML) decoder that can be shown using the random coding union bound, e.g., in [13, 2.6.4]. In the latter case, (32) is achieved by a single Q Y | X -based channel coding scheme in which the parameter t only exists in the analysis and is not an actual parameter of the code construction. We call this a single-scheme achievability of the GMI, and it follows from the fact that the Q Y | X -based ML decoding rule arg max j Q Y | X ( y | x j ) is mathematically equivalent to arg max j Q Y | X ( y | x j ) t for any t > 0 . In contrast, our Q Y | X -based JSCC using the Poisson functional representations does not straightforwardly admit the aforementioned mathematical equivalence as a proof device. Different values of t give different decoder-side kernels and, in general, different JSCC constructions. Hence, in our case, (32) should not be interpreted as the first-order channel-dependent achievable rate of a single Q Y | X -based JSCC design. Rather, it is a rate envelope over the t -parameterized family of ( t, Q Y | X ) -based JSCC designs. We call this scheme-envelope achievability . Note that such a distinction also has precedent in channel coding with mismatched decoding. For example, the achievability of the LM rate shown in [14, Theorem 5] via a cost-constrained random-coding ensemble is a scheme-envelope achievability because the parameters t and the auxiliary cost functions a ( · ) are code-design parameters. In contrast, for the case of finite alphabets, it is possible to prove a single-scheme achievability of the LM rate via constant-composition random coding (e.g., [13, 2.6.5]) using the metric-equivalence result in [13, Proposition 2.2]: on a constant-composition codebook, the decoding metrics q ( x, y ) and q ( x, y ) t e a ( x ) induce the same maximum-metric decoding rule for every t > 0 .

texts.orig: A. Definition of Second-Order Universality
texts.text: A. Definition of Second-Order Universality

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The notion of universal codes was first introduced in the source coding literature [19]. Universal source codes have two defining characteristics: they do not have knowledge of the source distribution, and they achieve the same asymptotic performance as optimal codes that do. For joint source-channel codes, we refer to the analogous first property as channelblindness . For the second property, we strengthen the usual notion from universal source coding: instead of requiring only first-order asymptotic optimality, we require the channel-blind JSCC construction to achieve both firstand second-order asymptotically optimal performance, matching an optimal channel-matched JSCC scheme. This is motivated by the fact that first-order optimality is already achieved by separate source-channel coding, so the study of first-order universal joint sourcechannel codes over a set of channels can simply be reduced to a universal channel coding problem.

In our second-order universal construction (next section), the channel-blind family of codes is generated by a common rule whose operating point ( n, k ) can be adjusted. That is, we provide a single code-generation rule over a set of channels that is reused across all ( n, k ) to obtain the channel-blind family of codes. This is in contrast to a purely existence result in which the codes U ( d ) n,k in the family could be unrelated across different blocklength pairs. In this sense, our universality result can be viewed as a form of construction-level separation: the rule used to generate the source-channel code is fixed uniformly over the set of channels, while channel adaptation is reduced to selecting the source blocklength k for a given channel blocklength n .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: V. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS
texts.text: V. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our mismatched-design result (Theorem 1) proves the scheme-envelope achievability of the generalized mutual information in the JSCC setting and for nonstationary channels over a fixed general alphabet. Theorem 1 also subsumes achievability results for nonstationary channels in the traditional matched JSCC. For future directions, one can study mismatched decoding involving an arbitrary decoding metric q ( x, y ) as well as derive the achievability of the LM rate in the JSCC framework. A possible step toward that extension is to choose a different Gibbs posterior in Definition 3. Furthermore, upgrading from scheme-envelope achievability to a single-scheme achievability of the GMI/LM rate in the JSCC setting is an interesting problem for future study.
texts.text: Our mismatched-design result (Theorem 1) proves the scheme-envelope achievability of the generalized mutual information in the JSCC setting and for nonstationary channels over a fixed general alphabet. Theorem 1 also subsumes achievability results for nonstationary channels in the traditional matched JSCC. For future directions, one can study mismatched decoding involving an arbitrary decoding metric q ( x, y ) as well as derive the achievability of the LM rate in the JSCC framework. A possible step toward that extension is to choose a different Gibbs posterior in Definition 3. Furthermore, upgrading from scheme-envelope achievability to a single-scheme achievability of the GMI/LM rate in the JSCC setting is an interesting problem for future study.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We also defined a new notion of universal JSCC and showed that a channel-blind family of JSCC is second-order universal over the set of memoryless and stationary block erasure channels with erasure probability p ∈ (0 , 1) . Extension to the nonstationary case is possible via a standard non-universal converse result for memoryless and nonstationary block erasure channels that matches the universal achievability result of Theorem 2.
